text:
Problem Overview

This problem has two parts. In the Initial Challenge, students analyze how percent changes based on how many in a total. The context is a student looking at his grades when he misses one point on every assignment. This relates to the idea of one away from the whole. As the whole changes with more points possible, the percent achieved increases.

In the What If...?, students analyze real-world applications of percent. There are six example cards that present different uses of percentages. small groups of students will analyze one of the examples and share results with the whole class.

Implementation Notes:

  • Have a class discussion about the Initial Challenge before moving to the What If...? situations.
  • What If...? Situation A—Assign one example to a small group based on needs you've collected through formative assessment observations in the unit. Each situation card has different considerations. This will allow you to differentiate the assigning of the tasks to the students. (Problem- Solving Environment)

Arc of Learning: Exploration, Analysis

Standards: See your state alignment chart.

NWDYK

How is percent used when making comparison statements? How are these the same as or different from other comparison statements?

Emerging Mathematical Ideas

Continue building expertise in applying multiple strategies to a variety of percent problems with increasing complexity.

Appreciate and notice

  • the power of percent ratios when comparing quantities relative to the total;
  • percents let you compare ratios by finding equivalent ratios for quantities out of 100; and
  • percent cannot be used to directly compare two quantities if they are not part of the same whole.

What If...? Situation A Examples

Sefina's Example This situation card needs extra consideration.

Students will have to consider if her phone is charges to 100% or not when she starts listening. The answer will depend on her starting battery charge percentage. So students will have to consider a range of possible answers. If she begins with a full battery:

3 hours for every 20% batter power used

×5 ↓ ↓×5

15 hours for every 100% battery power used

If she only started with 20% battery power, she would get the first 3 hours and no more listening time. So the listening time would range from 3 hours to 15 hours unless she recharges her battery.

Uyi's Example This might be the most difficult situation card.

Students will have to consider the population of a state to fully make sense of this situation. The state with 4% unemployment has 4 for every 100 people registered as unemployed. The state with 3% unemployment has 3 for every 100 people registered as unemployed.

Students should also consider that the size of the whole may change the number of people unemployed. Although the state with 4% has a higher percent of people unemployed, the number of people unemployed may be less than the other state.

For example, the 4% state might have a population of 3 million people. The 3% state might have a population of 20 million people. Even though the 4% is higher, fewer people will be registered as unemployed in that state.

So the consideration of the whole is important. the percent is the rte, but we need to know what it is the rate of.

Isabella's Example This situation card might be the least difficult card.

This card is for the students who need experience considering how a total of percentages for one event add to 100%. Students also need to compare the portions of different percentages.

Sara's Example The context in this situation card may have vocabulary that may limit some students' access to the questions. The mathematical computation will not be difficult. But there are questions to answer.

Students will need to read the situation closely to make sense of what is being asked. They will need to make sense of vocabulary such as accessible and van-accessible spot. the mathematics is not as difficult as other situation cards, but the reading may present a challenge. The context is real world, but it may be new to students.

Xavier's Example The context in this situation card may have vocabulary that may limit some students' access to the questions. The mathematics is as difficult as other cards. Students will find 2% of 5,000.

Students will need to read the situation closely to make sense of what is being asked. They will need to make sense of vocabulary such as credit card and cash back. The mathematics is not as difficult as other situation cards, but the reading may present a challenge. The context is real world, but it may be new to students.

Jacob's Example This situation card may fall in the middle of mathematical difficulty.

Students will need to make sense of 1/2 of a cup and 1/2 of a percent. To answer the question, students need to scale 8% 12.5 times to get to 100%. Then they will need to scale the 1/2 cup 12.5 times.
